prompts.text: Right shoulder: full passive range of movement (no joint restriction, no capsular tenderness); right arm: weakness of shoulder abduction (3/5), shoulder external rotation (3/5), and elbow flexion (4/5); wrist extension and finger movements fully preserved (5/5); biceps reflex reduced right; sensation mildly reduced over the right lateral arm; no skin vesicles; no Horner syndrome.
prompts.reasoning: Weakness of shoulder abduction (deltoid — axillary nerve/C5), external rotation (infraspinatus — suprascapular nerve/C5), and elbow flexion (biceps — musculocutaneous nerve/C5-C6), with preserved wrist and finger extension, localises to a C5-C6 distribution. Full passive shoulder range of motion excludes an intrinsic joint or rotator cuff lesion — if the shoulder joint were pathological, passive movement would also be restricted. The same distribution could arise from C5/C6 disc prolapse (cervical radiculopathy) or injury to the upper trunk of the brachial plexus. Absence of vesicles excludes herpes zoster. MRI cervical spine and neurophysiology are required to distinguish between these.

prompts.label: MRI Cervical Spine & Nerve Conduction Studies
prompts.text: MRI cervical spine: no disc prolapse, no foraminal stenosis at C5/C6, no cord signal change; nerve conduction studies: normal median and ulnar motor and sensory studies; EMG of right deltoid, supraspinatus, and infraspinatus: fibrillation potentials and positive sharp waves indicating acute denervation; reduced motor unit recruitment in right deltoid and biceps.
prompts.reasoning: Normal MRI cervical spine definitively excludes C5/C6 cervical radiculopathy from disc prolapse or foraminal stenosis — the most important structural differential. EMG findings of acute denervation (fibrillation potentials and positive sharp waves develop 2–3 weeks after axonal injury) affecting multiple muscles supplied by different peripheral nerves (deltoid — axillary nerve; supraspinatus/infraspinatus — suprascapular nerve) but all arising from the C5/C6 brachial plexus distribution indicates a multifocal plexopathy rather than a single root lesion. Normal nerve conduction studies suggest the lesion is primarily axonal (not demyelinating). The pattern of multifocal denervation within a plexus territory without a compressive lesion is characteristic of neuralgic amyotrophy. MRI brachial plexus provides definitive localisation.

prompts.label: MRI Brachial Plexus & Outcome
prompts.text: MRI brachial plexus: increased T2 signal and mild swelling in multiple individual nerve fascicles of the right brachial plexus, predominantly involving the suprascapular and axillary nerves; no compressive mass lesion; patchy, multifocal distribution not conforming to a single trunk or cord; diagnosis: brachial neuritis (Parsonage-Turner syndrome); short course of prednisolone for residual pain; physiotherapy commenced.
prompts.reasoning: MRI brachial plexus in neuralgic amyotrophy shows patchy, multifocal T2 hyperintensity and nerve enlargement — characteristically affecting individual fascicles of multiple different nerves in a non-anatomical (non-trunk, non-cord) distribution, which is the signature of an inflammatory process targeting specific fascicles rather than a compressive or traumatic lesion. No mass lesion excludes brachial plexus tumour, Pancoast tumour, or radiation plexopathy. Parsonage-Turner syndrome (neuralgic amyotrophy) is an acute immune-mediated brachial plexopathy. Pathology: focal inflammatory demyelination and axonal degeneration. SEPT9 gene mutations cause hereditary neuralgic amyotrophy (recurrent attacks). Prognosis: 80–90% recover fully or near-fully over 1–3 years. Physiotherapy prevents frozen shoulder. Short-course corticosteroids may reduce acute pain duration. Recurrence occurs in ~25%.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 42-year-old woman presents with a 4-month history of intermittent tingling and numbness in both hands that is troubling her sleep.
prompts.reasoning: Bilateral hand tingling and numbness in a middle-aged woman requires systematic evaluation. Carpal tunnel syndrome (CTS) is the most common entrapment neuropathy and a leading cause of nocturnal hand tingling. Cervical radiculopathy (C6 or C7) can cause hand sensory symptoms. Peripheral neuropathy produces bilateral distal sensory disturbance. Thoracic outlet syndrome (brachial plexus compression) causes hand and forearm symptoms. Raynaud phenomenon causes vasospastic colour changes rather than persistent neurological symptoms. The symptom distribution and timing will be the key discriminating axis.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: The tingling is worst at night and wakes her from sleep; she relieves it by shaking or hanging her hands over the side of the bed; during the day it occurs when holding a phone or steering wheel for a prolonged period.
prompts.reasoning: Nocturnal paraesthesiae that are relieved by shaking or dangling the hand (the 'flick sign') are characteristic of median nerve compression — the wrist is held in flexion during sleep, increasing carpal tunnel pressure and causing ischaemic compression of the median nerve. Peripheral neuropathy produces continuous symptoms without this postural/shaking relief pattern. Cervical radiculopathy is typically worsened by neck movement and Valsalva, not by wrist position. Thoracic outlet syndrome is provoked by overhead arm positioning and not specifically nocturnal. At least five diagnoses remain at this stage.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: She was diagnosed with hypothyroidism 2 years ago and takes levothyroxine; her most recent TSH was borderline elevated (5.8 mU/L) at her last check 6 months ago; she works as a physiotherapist and performs repetitive hand movements throughout the day; no diabetes, no pregnancy, no rheumatoid arthritis.
prompts.reasoning: Hypothyroidism is a recognised cause of carpal tunnel syndrome — myxoedematous infiltration of the flexor retinaculum and tenosynovium within the carpal tunnel increases tunnel pressure and compresses the median nerve. Borderline elevated TSH suggests suboptimal hypothyroid control, making a hypothyroid contribution plausible. However, hypothyroidism can also cause a generalised sensorimotor peripheral neuropathy distinct from CTS. Repetitive occupational hand movements are a risk factor for CTS but are insufficient alone to confirm the diagnosis. At least four diagnoses remain plausible pending physical examination.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Sensory testing: reduced pinprick sensation over the palmar surface of the thumb, index, and middle fingers bilaterally; normal sensation over the ring and little fingers and the dorsum of the hand; thenar muscle bulk appears mildly reduced on the right compared to the left; grip strength subjectively reduced right hand.
prompts.reasoning: Sensory loss restricted to the palmar surface of the thumb, index, and middle fingers — the median nerve sensory territory distal to the carpal tunnel — with sparing of the ring finger (dual innervation, median and ulnar) and little finger (ulnar) localises the lesion to the median nerve distal to the wrist. Crucially, normal sensation over the dorsum of the hand excludes a more proximal median nerve lesion (the palmar cutaneous branch, given off proximal to the carpal tunnel, supplies dorsal hand sensation — its preservation confirms the lesion is within the tunnel). Mild thenar wasting indicates motor involvement (abductor pollicis brevis — the most sensitive motor indicator of CTS). Cervical radiculopathy at C6/C7 would affect a dermatomal distribution including the forearm. Pronator teres syndrome compresses the median nerve in the forearm — sensory loss would include the palmar cutaneous branch territory.

prompts.label: Provocation Tests & Thyroid Function
prompts.text: Sustained wrist flexion at 90° for 60 seconds reproduces bilateral tingling in the median nerve territory within 30 seconds (positive test); sustained compression over the carpal tunnel at the wrist crease reproduces tingling in the same distribution bilaterally; TSH repeated: 6.1 mU/L (mildly elevated — undertreated hypothyroidism confirmed); free T4: low-normal.
prompts.reasoning: Sustained wrist flexion (Phalen's test) and direct carpal tunnel compression reproduce median nerve territory symptoms, providing clinical evidence of median nerve compression within the carpal tunnel. These tests have moderate sensitivity (~80% and ~64% respectively) and are clinically useful in context. Undertreated hypothyroidism (elevated TSH, low-normal T4) confirms a contributing cause — myxoedematous infiltration of the carpal tunnel contents is the mechanism. The distinction between primary CTS and hypothyroid-associated CTS matters clinically: optimising levothyroxine therapy should be the first intervention. Nerve conduction studies will confirm the electrophysiological diagnosis.

prompts.label: Nerve Conduction Studies
prompts.text: Bilateral nerve conduction studies: prolonged median nerve distal motor latencies (right 5.8 ms, left 5.2 ms; normal <4.2 ms); reduced median nerve sensory conduction velocities across the wrist; median sensory nerve action potential amplitudes reduced bilaterally; ulnar nerve conduction studies: entirely normal bilaterally; no evidence of generalised polyneuropathy.
prompts.reasoning: Nerve conduction studies are the gold standard investigation for CTS. Prolonged median distal motor latency and slowed sensory conduction velocity across the wrist confirm focal demyelination of the median nerve at the carpal tunnel. Normal ulnar nerve conduction excludes generalised peripheral neuropathy (which would affect both ulnar and median nerves). The focal nature of the conduction abnormality (across the wrist segment only, not in more proximal median nerve segments) confirms entrapment within the carpal tunnel rather than a more proximal median nerve lesion (pronator teres syndrome, C6/C7 radiculopathy). Management: levothyroxine dose optimisation first; wrist splinting at night; if persistent after thyroid optimisation — corticosteroid injection or surgical carpal tunnel decompression (division of the flexor retinaculum).

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 48-year-old man presents with a 3-month history of numbness and tingling in his left hand, predominantly affecting the little finger and the inner side of the ring finger.
prompts.reasoning: Sensory symptoms in the little and ring fingers correspond to the ulnar nerve distribution (C8/T1). The differential must include ulnar neuropathy at the elbow (cubital tunnel) or wrist (Guyon's canal), C8/T1 cervical radiculopathy, lower trunk brachial plexopathy, thoracic outlet syndrome, and in a middle-aged man, a Pancoast tumour at the lung apex. Carpal tunnel syndrome predominantly affects the thumb, index, and middle fingers — ring and little finger symptoms make it less likely but it remains in the differential until the distribution is confirmed. The anatomical localisation along the ulnar/C8-T1 axis is established but the level of the lesion is not yet determined.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: He works as an accountant and spends long hours at a desk with his elbows resting on a hard surface; the tingling is worse when he is working or holds his elbow bent for extended periods, and he has noticed the little finger seems to 'drift away' when he places his hand flat on the table.
prompts.reasoning: Prolonged elbow flexion and direct pressure on the medial elbow (leaning on hard surfaces) are the classic risk factors for ulnar neuropathy at the elbow — elbow flexion increases tension on the ulnar nerve at the cubital tunnel and narrows the cubital tunnel. Little finger abduction at rest ('wartenberg sign' — the little finger drifts into abduction due to weakness of the palmar interosseous muscle) suggests motor involvement of the ulnar nerve's intrinsic hand muscles. Carpal tunnel syndrome is now less likely given the ulnar finger distribution and motor sign. C8/T1 radiculopathy and thoracic outlet syndrome remain plausible as they share the same dermatomal territory. Pancoast tumour becomes less likely without constitutional symptoms or shoulder pain.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: He had a left elbow fracture 15 years ago that was treated conservatively; he has no diabetes, no thyroid disease, and no family history of neuropathy; no alcohol use; no recent weight loss or respiratory symptoms.
prompts.reasoning: A prior elbow fracture is a significant risk factor for ulnar neuropathy — post-traumatic cubitus valgus deformity (from malunion or overgrowth) causes progressive stretching and angulation of the ulnar nerve around the medial epicondyle, a phenomenon termed 'tardy ulnar palsy'. This history raises the probability of ulnar neuropathy at the elbow substantially. Absence of diabetes, thyroid disease, and alcohol reduces the probability of generalised peripheral neuropathy. No weight loss or respiratory symptoms makes a Pancoast tumour less likely. C8/T1 radiculopathy from a cervical disc prolapse remains in the differential — a prior elbow injury does not preclude concurrent cervical pathology.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Reduced pinprick sensation over the little finger and the ulnar half of the ring finger, and over the hypothenar eminence on the palmar surface; weakness of left hand intrinsic muscles (interossei — finger abduction/adduction 3/5); mild hypothenar wasting; wrist flexion and finger flexion (to the little finger) preserved; no Horner syndrome; cervical spine: full range of motion, no radicular signs on neck movement.
prompts.reasoning: The sensory loss pattern — palmar surface of little finger, ulnar half of ring finger, and hypothenar eminence — matches the ulnar nerve distribution distal to the dorsal cutaneous branch take-off point. Preserved wrist flexion (flexor carpi ulnaris) and preserved finger flexion to the little finger (flexor digitorum profundus — innervated proximal to most common compression sites) provides initial localisation information but does not yet confirm elbow versus wrist level. Intrinsic hand muscle weakness (interossei — pure hand branch) is present at both elbow and wrist lesions. Hypothenar wasting indicates established denervation. Normal cervical spine movements without radicular reproduction argue against C8/T1 radiculopathy. Lower trunk brachial plexus lesion remains — both motor and sensory involvement across the C8/T1 territory. Neurophysiology is required to localise.

prompts.label: Nerve Conduction Studies
prompts.text: Ulnar nerve motor conduction studies: conduction velocity across the elbow segment 34 m/s (normal >50 m/s) — significantly slowed; conduction velocity in the forearm segment: 52 m/s (normal); focal conduction block across the elbow on stimulation above versus below the medial epicondyle; sensory conduction: reduced SNAP amplitude and slowed velocity in the little finger; median nerve studies: normal.
prompts.reasoning: Focal slowing of ulnar nerve conduction velocity specifically across the elbow segment (34 m/s across elbow, normal velocity in the forearm) with a conduction block localises the site of nerve compression to the cubital tunnel at the elbow. This pattern of focal demyelination at the elbow is the defining NCS finding of cubital tunnel syndrome (ulnar neuropathy at the elbow). If the compression were at Guyon's canal (wrist), slowing would be seen in the wrist-to-palm segment, not across the elbow. C8/T1 radiculopathy would show abnormal F-waves and paraspinal EMG changes without focal slowing at the elbow. Normal median nerve studies confirm the process is not a generalised neuropathy. The tardy ulnar palsy from the prior fracture is the likely mechanism.

prompts.label: Ultrasound & Management
prompts.text: Ultrasound of the left ulnar nerve at the cubital tunnel: enlarged, swollen nerve cross-sectional area of 14 mm² (normal <10 mm²) at the medial epicondyle; post-traumatic cubitus valgus deformity confirmed on X-ray of the left elbow; surgical anterior transposition of the ulnar nerve performed; at 3 months: sensation recovering, intrinsic muscle strength improving to 4/5.
prompts.reasoning: Ultrasound demonstrating nerve enlargement at the medial epicondyle confirms the anatomical site of compression and the inflammatory/compressive neuropathy. Cubitus valgus (lateral deviation of the forearm beyond normal carrying angle) from the old fracture causes progressive stretching and friction of the ulnar nerve at the medial epicondyle with elbow flexion — the mechanism of 'tardy ulnar palsy'. Surgical anterior transposition moves the nerve from the posterior aspect of the medial epicondyle to the anterior aspect, removing the tensioning mechanism. Conservative management (elbow padding, avoiding prolonged flexion, night splinting) is appropriate for mild cases. Surgical outcomes for cubital tunnel syndrome are good — most patients recover full or near-full function if decompressed before significant axonal loss occurs.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 29-year-old man presents to the emergency department with a right-sided wrist drop that he noticed upon waking this morning.
prompts.reasoning: Wrist drop (inability to extend the wrist) in a young man developing acutely overnight encompasses a broad differential. Radial nerve palsy is the most common peripheral nerve cause — the radial nerve is vulnerable to compression in the spiral groove of the humerus. Posterior interosseous nerve (deep radial branch) palsy causes finger drop without wrist drop. C7 radiculopathy can cause wrist extension weakness but would also affect triceps. A brachial plexus posterior cord lesion would affect radial, axillary, and upper subscapular nerve distributions simultaneously. Ischaemic stroke affecting the contralateral motor cortex is a less likely but important exclusion in a young adult.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: He went to bed in his usual good health following a party where he consumed a large quantity of alcohol; he woke with the right arm numb and unable to extend his wrist or fingers; he denies any trauma, injection, or tourniquet use on the arm.
prompts.reasoning: Alcohol intoxication followed by overnight immobility in an awkward position — typically with the arm compressed over a bench, chair back, or partner — is the classic mechanism of radial nerve compression in the spiral groove of the humerus ('Saturday night palsy'). The radial nerve is particularly vulnerable at the spiral groove where it is superficial and closely adherent to the humeral periosteum. Prolonged compression causes focal demyelination (neurapraxia) and in severe cases axonal injury. Absence of trauma, injection, or tourniquet reduces the probability of more proximal injury or iatrogenic nerve damage. C7 radiculopathy from a cervical disc prolapse would be expected to have a more gradual onset and associated neck pain.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: He is otherwise fit and healthy; no prior neurological episodes; no family history of neuropathy; no diabetes or autoimmune conditions; social alcohol use — approximately 20 units last evening; no medications.
prompts.reasoning: The history of 20 units of alcohol and overnight positional immobility is highly consistent with compressive radial neuropathy at the spiral groove. No prior episodes excludes hereditary neuropathy with liability to pressure palsies (HNPP) on history alone — though a single episode in isolation cannot exclude HNPP. No diabetes reduces the likelihood of diabetic mononeuropathy. C7 radiculopathy from acute disc prolapse typically presents with neck pain, often precipitated by Valsalva or axial loading — an overnight alcohol-associated onset is atypical. The physical examination will determine whether triceps function is preserved, which is critical for localising the level of radial nerve injury.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Right wrist extension: absent (wrist drops into flexion); right finger extension at the MCP joints: absent; right brachioradialis reflex: absent; right triceps power: 5/5; right triceps reflex: present and normal; sensory loss over the dorsal aspect of the right thumb and first web space; no Horner syndrome; cervical spine: full range, no radicular reproduction.
prompts.reasoning: Preserved triceps power and triceps reflex is the critical finding for localisation. The triceps is innervated by radial nerve branches given off in the axilla and upper arm, before the nerve enters the spiral groove. Therefore, a spiral groove lesion spares the triceps — preserved triceps with wrist drop localises the radial nerve injury to the spiral groove or below. A lesion in the axilla (e.g., crutch palsy) or from a posterior cord lesion would also affect the triceps (triceps weakness + wrist drop). Absent brachioradialis reflex confirms radial nerve involvement below the axillary branches. Sensory loss over the first dorsal web space corresponds to the superficial radial nerve — its involvement confirms this is the main radial nerve trunk, not just the posterior interosseous nerve (which is a pure motor branch with no sensory territory).

prompts.label: Nerve Conduction Studies
prompts.text: Radial nerve motor conduction studies: conduction block with reduced compound muscle action potential (CMAP) amplitude when stimulating proximal to the spiral groove versus distal; no significant conduction velocity slowing (neurapraxic pattern without axonal loss); sensory studies: absent superficial radial SNAP on right; cervical spine MRI: no disc prolapse, no foraminal stenosis at C7.
prompts.reasoning: A neurapraxic conduction block pattern (reduced CMAP amplitude with proximal stimulation compared to distal stimulation, without significant conduction velocity slowing) confirms focal demyelination at the spiral groove level — consistent with compressive injury from prolonged external pressure. Neurapraxia (focal demyelination without axonal transection) has an excellent prognosis — full recovery expected within 6–12 weeks as remyelination occurs. Axonotmesis (axonal disruption) would show reduced CMAP with both proximal and distal stimulation, with denervation on EMG and a longer (3–6 months) recovery period. Normal cervical MRI excludes C7 radiculopathy. The conduction block localised to the spiral groove segment definitively confirms the diagnosis.

prompts.label: EMG & Prognosis
prompts.text: EMG of extensor digitorum communis and extensor carpi radialis: no fibrillation potentials or positive sharp waves (consistent with neurapraxia — no axonal loss); normal motor unit morphology; EMG of triceps: normal; at 6 weeks: wrist extension returning with MRC grade 3/5; at 10 weeks: full wrist extension (5/5) recovered; diagnosis: compressive radial neuropathy at the spiral groove (neurapraxia), consistent with Saturday night palsy.
prompts.reasoning: Absence of fibrillation potentials on EMG confirms neurapraxia (focal demyelination without axonal injury) — fibrillation potentials (spontaneous discharges from denervated muscle fibres) appear 2–3 weeks after axonal injury and are absent in pure neurapraxia. Normal triceps EMG confirms the lesion is below the spiral groove branches, consistent with the examination findings. Recovery within 6–10 weeks is typical of neurapraxia at this site. Management: wrist drop splint to maintain functional hand position and prevent contracture; physiotherapy; protection of the arm from further compression; patient education regarding alcohol-related positional injury risk. Most cases of Saturday night palsy recover completely within 3 months.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 38-year-old male warehouse worker presents with a 3-week history of severe lower back pain radiating into the right leg.
prompts.reasoning: Lower back pain radiating into the leg is a common presentation with a broad differential. Lumbar disc prolapse compressing a nerve root (most commonly L4/L5 or L5/S1) is the most frequent cause of radicular leg pain in middle-aged adults with physical occupations. Lumbar canal stenosis causes neurogenic claudication, typically in older patients. Piriformis syndrome and sacroiliac joint dysfunction can produce referred lower limb pain without true radiculopathy. Cauda equina syndrome — a surgical emergency — must be actively excluded. Spinal metastasis must be considered in patients with constitutional symptoms. The character, distribution, and associated neurological symptoms will determine the priority of each diagnosis.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: The leg pain radiates from the right buttock through the posterior thigh and into the posterolateral calf and outer border of the foot; it is sharp, burning, and electric in character; it is worsened by sitting, coughing, and bending forward, but partially relieved by lying flat; no bladder or bowel disturbance, no bilateral leg symptoms.
prompts.reasoning: The distribution — posterior thigh, posterolateral calf, outer foot — corresponds to the S1 nerve root territory (L5 would involve the dorsum of the foot and great toe). Sharp, burning, electric radicular pain worsened by sitting (which increases disc pressure and sciatic nerve tension), coughing (Valsalva raises intradiscal pressure), and forward flexion — with relief on lying flat — is the classic pattern of discogenic nerve root compression. Lumbar canal stenosis causes bilateral, activity-induced leg pain (neurogenic claudication) that is relieved by sitting or forward flexion — the opposite pattern. The absence of bladder/bowel symptoms and bilateral involvement effectively reduces the concern for cauda equina syndrome, which remains a differential to actively exclude on examination.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: He has no prior back or neurological history; the onset followed lifting heavy pallets at work 3 weeks ago; no weight loss, no night sweats, no prior malignancy, no recent fever or infection; no steroid use; BMI 31 kg/m².
prompts.reasoning: The onset following heavy lifting is consistent with a posterior disc prolapse — axial loading combined with forward flexion increases intradiscal pressure and can cause the nucleus pulposus to herniate through the annulus fibrosus posterolaterally, compressing the adjacent nerve root in the lateral recess or intervertebral foramen. Absence of constitutional symptoms (weight loss, night sweats), prior malignancy, and fever effectively excludes spinal metastasis and infective discitis/spondylodiscitis. Normal immune status reduces the probability of atypical infection. Piriformis syndrome and sacroiliac joint dysfunction remain plausible differentials for referred pain but would not produce true neurological radiculopathy — examination will discriminate.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Straight leg raise: positive on the right at 40° — reproduces the radiating leg pain (not just back pain); positive crossed straight leg raise (raising the contralateral leg reproduces right leg symptoms); right ankle jerk: absent; right ankle plantar flexion: 4/5 (mildly reduced); pinprick reduced over the right lateral foot and little toe; bladder and bowel function intact; perianal sensation intact.
prompts.reasoning: A positive straight leg raise (SLR) at 40° reproducing radiating leg pain (not simply back pain) is a highly specific sign of lumbar nerve root tension — it stretches the sciatic nerve and its nerve roots, and reproduction of radicular pain at less than 60° is highly specific for nerve root compression. The crossed SLR (raising the contralateral leg reproduces ipsilateral symptoms) is the most specific sign for a large central or paracentral disc herniation. Absent right ankle jerk (S1 reflex arc) and mild ankle plantar flexion weakness (S1 myotome — gastrocnemius/soleus) with sensory loss over the lateral foot (S1 dermatome) confirms right S1 radiculopathy. Piriformis syndrome can produce a positive SLR but does not cause reflex loss or myotomal weakness. Intact perianal sensation and bladder/bowel function effectively exclude cauda equina syndrome.

prompts.label: MRI Lumbar Spine
prompts.text: MRI lumbar spine: right posterolateral disc herniation at L5/S1 with significant compression of the right S1 nerve root in the lateral recess; disc material is hypointense (desiccated/degenerate); mild epidural fat effacement at this level; no cord signal change; no spinal metastasis; no cauda equina compression.
prompts.reasoning: MRI confirms a right posterolateral L5/S1 disc herniation causing right S1 nerve root compression — consistent with the clinical picture of S1 radiculopathy (absent ankle jerk, plantar flexion weakness, lateral foot sensory loss). Piriformis syndrome has no structural nerve root compression on MRI — its exclusion by MRI confirming the disc herniation as the sole structural explanation is definitive. Disc herniation at L5/S1 is the most common level for lumbar disc prolapse (the L5/S1 junction bears the highest axial load). A desiccated disc (hypointense on T2) reflects chronic degeneration — the acute prolapse occurred through a pre-existing degenerate disc. No evidence of cauda equina compression confirms that urgent surgery is not required at this time.

prompts.label: Management & Outcome
prompts.text: Conservative management commenced: selective NSAIDs, physiotherapy with specific lumbar stabilisation exercises, and activity modification; at 6 weeks: significant improvement in leg pain (VAS 7/10 → 2/10); ankle reflex remains absent but plantar flexion strength fully recovered (5/5); repeat MRI at 3 months: disc material has partially resorbed; diagnosis: sciatica from right S1 radiculopathy secondary to L5/S1 posterolateral disc herniation.
prompts.reasoning: Symptomatic improvement with conservative management and radiological evidence of disc resorption confirms the diagnosis and the natural history of lumbar disc herniation — approximately 90% of disc herniations resorb spontaneously over 6–12 weeks as the extruded nucleus pulposus is phagocytosed by macrophages. Persistent absent ankle reflex despite motor and sensory recovery reflects the slower regeneration of myelinated reflex arc fibres compared to larger motor axons and is common after S1 radiculopathy. Surgical intervention (microdiscectomy) is indicated for cauda equina syndrome (emergency), failed conservative management after 6–12 weeks, or progressive neurological deficit. The diagnosis is sciatica — radicular leg pain in the sciatic nerve distribution caused by lumbar disc herniation with nerve root compression.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 44-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department with acute severe lower back pain and bilateral leg weakness that developed over the past 12 hours.
prompts.reasoning: Acute severe back pain with bilateral leg weakness is a potential neurological emergency requiring immediate assessment. Cauda equina syndrome (CES) — compression of the lumbar and sacral nerve roots below the conus — is a surgical emergency and must be excluded first. Conus medullaris syndrome involves the terminal spinal cord. Transverse myelitis and spinal cord compression (from disc, tumour, or haematoma) can produce bilateral leg dysfunction. Aortic dissection can cause paraplegia from anterior spinal artery compromise and back pain. The speed of onset, presence of sphincter involvement, and neurological distribution are the critical axes for discrimination.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: Over the past 8 hours she has also developed difficulty initiating urination and reduced sensation when wiping after toileting; she had a 2-week history of right-sided back and leg pain before today's acute deterioration.
prompts.reasoning: Urinary retention (difficulty initiating urination) combined with reduced perineal sensation and bilateral leg weakness developing acutely on a background of unilateral radicular symptoms constitutes the clinical syndrome of cauda equina compression until proved otherwise — this is a neurosurgical emergency. Reduced perineal sensation (the 'saddle area' — perineum, inner thighs, perianal skin) is supplied by sacral nerve roots S3-S5 and is a hallmark feature distinguishing CES from a simple bilateral radiculopathy. The 2-week history of right-sided radicular symptoms (consistent with initial posterolateral disc bulge) followed by acute bilateral deterioration suggests central disc herniation with acute expansion compressing the cauda equina. The diagnosis must be confirmed or excluded with immediate MRI.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: She has a 2-year history of intermittent lower back pain managed conservatively; no prior neurological diagnoses; no malignancy, no anticoagulant use, no recent trauma; she takes ibuprofen occasionally for back pain; no steroid use.
prompts.reasoning: Chronic intermittent back pain in a middle-aged woman is consistent with degenerative lumbar disc disease — the commonest substrate for acute disc herniation causing CES. No anticoagulant use reduces the probability of an acute spinal epidural haematoma (though not eliminates it). No malignancy reduces the probability of spinal metastatic compression as the cause, though this cannot be fully excluded until imaging. No prior neurological episodes argue against an acute relapse of a demyelinating condition (MS-related transverse myelitis). The most likely mechanism remains acute central disc herniation at the L4/5 or L5/S1 level, causing compression of the cauda equina. Urgent MRI is the only investigation that can adequately characterise the level and cause.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Bilateral lower limb weakness: hip flexion 3/5, knee extension 4/5, ankle dorsiflexion 2/5 bilaterally; bilateral absent ankle jerks; bilateral extensor plantar responses absent (plantars downgoing); reduced pinprick bilaterally in a saddle distribution (perineum, inner buttocks, posterior thighs); urinary retention confirmed on bladder scan (450 mL); rectal tone reduced on digital rectal examination.
prompts.reasoning: Saddle anaesthesia (perineum and inner thighs), bilateral LMN signs (flaccid weakness, absent ankle jerks, absent plantar responses), urinary retention, and reduced rectal tone constitute the full clinical syndrome of cauda equina compression. Pure LMN signs throughout distinguish CES from conus medullaris syndrome, which characteristically produces a mixed UMN and LMN picture (the conus contains the cord — UMN pathways — as well as the sacral root origin — LMN features). Downgoing plantars (or absent plantars) in CES reflect absent reflex arc, not an intact pyramidal tract. Conus medullaris syndrome would be expected to produce extensor plantars (positive Babinski) due to involvement of the corticospinal tract within the conus. MRI is required to determine the exact level and aetiology.

prompts.label: Emergency MRI Spine
prompts.text: Emergency MRI lumbar spine: large central L4/L5 disc herniation with complete compression of the thecal sac; the herniated disc material extends over 15 mm craniocaudally, obliterating the epidural fat and compressing multiple lumbar and sacral nerve roots; no spinal cord signal change (conus at T12/L1 — above the level of compression); no metastatic lesion; no epidural haematoma.
prompts.reasoning: The MRI demonstrates massive central L4/L5 disc herniation compressing the cauda equina — confirming CES and excluding conus medullaris syndrome (the conus at T12/L1 is above the compression, and no cord signal change is present). Absence of metastatic disease and epidural haematoma narrows the aetiology to mechanical disc herniation. The thecal sac obliteration indicates high-grade compression of multiple sacral nerve roots supplying sphincter function and perineal sensation. This is a neurosurgical emergency: immediate decompression (emergency microdiscectomy or lumbar decompression) is required. Outcome of sphincter function correlates strongly with time to decompression — operation within 24–48 hours of onset of urinary retention offers the best chance of sphincter recovery.

prompts.label: Emergency Surgery & Outcome
prompts.text: Emergency L4/L5 microdiscectomy performed within 6 hours of MRI; intraoperative findings: large extruded disc fragment compressing the entire cauda equina; at 48 hours post-operatively: bilateral ankle dorsiflexion improving (3/5); urinary catheter removed on day 3 — spontaneous voiding re-established with residual volumes <100 mL; saddle sensation partially recovering; at 3 months: independent walking with mild bilateral foot drop persisting.
prompts.reasoning: The surgical findings confirm massive disc herniation as the mechanical cause of CES. Prompt surgical decompression (within 24 hours of onset of urinary retention) achieves the best sphincter recovery outcomes — the 'incomplete' CES (some preserved sacral function) has a significantly better prognosis than 'complete' CES (total loss of saddle sensation and sphincter function). Re-establishment of voluntary voiding is a positive prognostic indicator. Residual foot drop (ankle dorsiflexion weakness) reflects the severity of L4/L5 nerve root axonal injury — motor axons require 3–6 months to regenerate from the compression site to the muscle. CES from disc herniation has a better prognosis than CES from malignancy or ischaemia. Long-term urological and physiotherapy follow-up is essential.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 31-year-old man is brought to the emergency department following a fall from height, with inability to move his legs and loss of bladder and bowel control.
prompts.reasoning: Acute paraplegia with sphincter loss following axial trauma is a spinal cord or cauda equina injury until proven otherwise. The injury level — thoracic cord, conus medullaris (T12/L1), or cauda equina (below L1) — determines the clinical syndrome and rehabilitation prognosis. Complete thoracic cord injury causes spastic paraplegia (UMN) below the lesion. Conus medullaris syndrome (at T12/L1) causes a mixed UMN and LMN pattern. Cauda equina syndrome (below L1) produces pure LMN signs. Anterior cord syndrome causes motor and pain/temperature loss with preserved proprioception. Central cord syndrome is more common in cervical injuries. Immediate imaging and neurological examination are required to localise the injury.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: He fell approximately 5 metres from scaffolding onto his lower back; he is haemodynamically stable but describes a complete inability to move or feel both legs, and has had no urine output since the injury 2 hours ago; he also reports no sensation when the perianal area is touched.
prompts.reasoning: A fall onto the lower back implicates the thoracolumbar junction (T12/L1) as the most vulnerable region — it is the transition zone between the rigid thoracic spine (supported by the rib cage) and the mobile lumbar spine, and is the most common site for compression fractures from axial loading. Urinary retention within 2 hours of injury and absent perianal sensation indicate sacral nerve root involvement (S3-S5 — the sacral parasympathetic outflow and pudendal sensory fibres) either from conus or cauda equina injury. The examination findings — specifically whether UMN or LMN signs predominate, and whether the Babinski response is present or absent — will be the critical discriminating step. At least four diagnoses remain plausible.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: No prior neurological or spinal history; no anticoagulant use; no prior malignancy or infection; previously physically active, working in construction; no alcohol or drug use at the time of the injury.
prompts.reasoning: No anticoagulant use reduces the probability of a spinal epidural haematoma as a contributing cause. No malignancy excludes pathological fracture at the injury site as the primary mechanism. The injury mechanism — axial loading from a fall onto the lower back — is consistent with a burst fracture at the thoracolumbar junction, which can simultaneously injure the conus medullaris (within the spinal canal at T12/L1) and the uppermost cauda equina roots (which exit from the conus). A pure fall mechanism without twisting reduces the probability of a rotational dislocation injury, which more commonly causes complete cord injuries at thoracic levels. Physical examination is required to determine the UMN/LMN pattern.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Bilateral lower limb power 0/5 throughout; bilateral ankle jerks: absent; bilateral knee jerks: brisk (2+ to 3+); bilateral extensor plantar responses (upgoing Babinski) bilaterally; saddle area sensory testing: absent to pinprick in the perineum and posterior thighs bilaterally; point of maximal spinal tenderness at the thoracolumbar junction; upper limb neurological examination: entirely normal.
prompts.reasoning: The mixed UMN and LMN neurological picture is the defining feature of conus medullaris syndrome and the critical discriminating finding from pure CES. Brisk knee jerks (L3/L4 reflex arc — preserved, indicating UMN influence on the spinal cord above L3/L4 is intact) combined with absent ankle jerks (S1 reflex arc — absent, indicating direct injury to the S1 roots at the conus level) and positive Babinski responses (indicating intact but damaged corticospinal tract within the conus) constitute the hallmark mixed picture. Pure cauda equina syndrome would produce globally absent reflexes (all LMN) and absent, never extensor, plantar responses. The level of tenderness at the thoracolumbar junction is anatomically consistent with a conus lesion. MRI is required urgently.

prompts.label: Emergency CT & MRI Spine
prompts.text: CT thoracolumbar spine: burst fracture of L1 with posterior retropulsion of a bony fragment into the spinal canal, causing approximately 50% canal compromise at the L1 level; MRI spine confirms bone fragment compressing the conus medullaris at T12/L1; T2 signal change within the conus (cord contusion/oedema); no epidural haematoma; no disc herniation at other levels.
prompts.reasoning: CT demonstrates a burst fracture at L1 — the anatomical level of the conus medullaris (the terminal end of the spinal cord, typically at T12–L1 in adults). Posterior bony retropulsion into the spinal canal is the mechanism of conus compression. MRI confirms intrinsic cord injury (T2 signal change = cord contusion or oedema) within the conus — distinguishing this from a purely cauda equina lesion (which would show no cord signal change). The T2 hyperintensity within the conus indicates primary cord injury, explaining the UMN features (positive Babinski, brisk knee jerks). Simultaneous injury to the exiting L1/S1 nerve roots (cauda equina roots beginning their descent) explains the LMN features (absent ankle jerks, saddle anaesthesia). This is the classic radiological confirmation of conus medullaris syndrome.

prompts.label: Surgical Decompression & Prognosis
prompts.text: Emergency posterior decompressive surgery and spinal stabilisation performed within 8 hours; intraoperative findings: L1 burst fracture with bony fragment indenting the conus; cord contusion without complete transection; at 6 weeks: bilateral knee extension partially recovering (2/5), ankle jerks remain absent, Babinski responses persist, voluntary bladder contractions absent (neurogenic bladder); at 6 months: knee extension 3/5, intermittent claudicant bladder function with clean intermittent catheterisation required; diagnosis confirmed: conus medullaris syndrome secondary to L1 burst fracture.
prompts.reasoning: The conus medullaris syndrome is characterised by its mixed UMN (corticospinal tract injury within the conus → spasticity, extensor plantars, upper reflex preservation) and LMN (sacral motor neuron and root injury within the conus → flaccid bladder, bowel, and sexual dysfunction; absent ankle jerks) picture. Sphincter dysfunction — typically a neurogenic (flaccid or acontractile) bladder and reduced rectal tone — is an early and prominent feature, often disproportionate to the limb motor deficit. Recovery is variable and often incomplete — the conus contains both motor neuron cell bodies (which cannot regenerate if destroyed) and ascending/descending tracts (which can recover partially). Long-term prognosis for sphincter function is generally worse than for limb motor recovery. Management includes urological rehabilitation, bowel programme, physiotherapy, and psychological support.
